body:
Title: CRA RC4210 2026 provincial and territorial credits combined with CGEB payments.

Source:
- https://www.canada.ca/en/revenue-agency/services/forms-publications/publications/rc4210/gst-hst-credit.html

CRA RC4210 states that provincial and territorial credit payments may be combined with Canada Groceries and Essentials Benefit payments.

The New Brunswick harmonized sales tax credit provides a maximum annual amount of $300 for an individual, $300 for a spouse or common-law partner, and $100 per child under 19 years of age, with $300 for the first child in a single parent family. The credit is reduced by 2% of adjusted family net income over $35,000.

The Newfoundland and Labrador income supplement maximum annual payment amount is $520 for a single individual, $589 for an individual with a spouse or common-law partner, plus $231 per child under 19 years of age. The Newfoundland and Labrador seniors' benefit provides a non-taxable annual amount of $1,882 for a single senior or a married or common-law couple with at least one senior whose adjusted family net income is $30,409 or less. Eligible seniors may get part of the payment if adjusted family net income is between $30,409 and $46,549.

The Nova Scotia affordable living tax credit provides a maximum annual credit of $255 for an individual or couple and $60 for each child under 19 years of age. The credit is reduced by 5% of adjusted family net income over $30,000.

The Ontario sales tax credit provides a maximum annual credit of $378 for each adult and each child under 19 years of age in a family. A single individual with no children is reduced by 4% of adjusted net income over $29,047. A single parent, married person, or common-law family is reduced by 4% of adjusted family net income over $36,309. The amount could be calculated using a seniors' income threshold of $37,273 if the recipient is married, has a qualified dependant, or both, and is 64 years old or older on December 31 of the applicable tax year.

The Prince Edward Island sales tax credit provides an annual credit of $310 for an individual plus, if applicable, $55 for a spouse, common-law partner, or eligible dependant. Starting in November 2026 the credit will be renamed the Prince Edward Island essentials benefit and a new minimum payment amount of $175 will be introduced.

The Saskatchewan low-income tax credit provides $460 for an individual, $460 for a spouse or common-law partner or eligible dependant, and $181 per child under 19 years of age, with a maximum of 2 children, or an annual credit of up to $1,282 per family. The credit starts to be reduced when adjusted family net income is more than $39,345. Families with adjusted family net income between $39,345 and $81,668 may get part of the credit.
